core_behavior.note: All three models define 'invalid user' and 'gracefully' differently without a shared basis, and none flags that both terms are undefined in the requirement.

inputs.note: Model B enumerates twelve distinct invalid-user input categories; Models A and C list only basic credential and identifier types.

outputs.note: Model B specifies granular HTTP status codes and structured JSON error bodies per scenario; Models A and C describe generic error messages and redirects without HTTP codes.

assumptions.note: Model B has an empty assumptions array while Models A and C each define what 'invalid user' means; none flags that the requirement leaves both key terms undefined.

failure_modes.note: Model B lists failure modes as a restatement of invalid-user input categories; Models A and C describe system-level failures such as database unavailability and unexpected errors.

edge_cases.note: Model B focuses on malformed identifiers and cross-tenant access; Model A addresses account lifecycle scenarios; Model C focuses on input format issues and injection attempts.

security_constraints.note: Model A specifies HTTPS/TLS, salted password hashing, and brute-force limits; Model B focuses on non-leakage and logging; Model C specifies input sanitization and rate limiting without transport or storage requirements.

performance_constraints.note: Model B has an empty performance_constraints array while Models A and C specify latency and concurrency requirements.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in six of the eight fields, reflecting that the underspecified requirement allowed each model to independently introduce different design choices.
